title: The Wolf's Deposition
subtitle: A forty-year institutional record, captured one answer at a time.
description: Pulp Fiction-inspired deposition framing for preserving William Sandhu’s institutional knowledge in his own words.

prompts.text: Describe your first day managing a Jack in the Box in Brooklyn. What did the physical space look like and who was already there when you arrived?
You went from managing fast food in New York to IT systems analysis at SMU in Dallas. What happened in between that made that jump feel logical?
Who was the most technically capable person you worked with at SMU, and what did they know that you did not?
When did you first understand that IT was not a support function but a strategic one? What triggered that realization?
Describe the IT infrastructure at Lone Star Gas in 1979. What was the oldest system still running and what was everyone pretending still worked?
What was the unofficial rule everyone followed at Maxus Energy that nobody ever wrote down?
You spent years in the energy sector before moving to law firm IT at Jones Day. What drove that move and what surprised you most about legal IT?
What did the server room at Jones Day look like in 1987? How many people understood what was actually in it?
Who was the senior partner at Jones Day who most understood technology, and how did they use that understanding?
Law firms run on billable hours. How did that model shape every IT decision you made, even when it should not have?
You ran IT at both Jones Day and ARCO during overlapping years. How did those two environments differ in their relationship to technology?
Describe a specific room at ARCO — a data center, a conference room, a drilling site — that captured what the company was in that era.
What was the moment at ARCO when you understood that oil companies were going to be late to every major technology shift?
What was the thing everyone at ARCO knew about their IT infrastructure that nobody in the executive suite was willing to hear?
Describe what the HP culture felt like before the Agilent split — the campus, the unwritten rules, the rituals. What made it distinctly HP?
HP spun off Agilent in 1999. Were people surprised? Who saw it coming and who did not?
Name the most technically dangerous person you worked with at Agilent — someone who could see around corners everyone else thought were walls.
SS7 was the backbone of telecom signaling in the 1990s. Describe a room — a NOC, a carrier facility, a lab — where you saw that infrastructure up close.
Telecom in the late 1990s was running on infrastructure older than most of the people maintaining it. What was the real maintenance doctrine everyone followed unofficially?
When did you first understand that the telecom industry was going to collapse under its own infrastructure debt? Was there a specific meeting, a number, a customer?
You helped build HP's software development center in Pudong, Shanghai. What was the conversation that made that happen and who in the room was most skeptical?
Describe the HP software lab in Chennai. How many people, what kind of work, what was the physical experience of managing a 300-person collaboration center in India?
What did the Indian engineers at TCS know about the HP systems they were maintaining that their HP counterparts in the US did not?
Who was your most important counterpart on the TCS side in Chennai, and what did working with them teach you about how large organizations actually transfer knowledge?
Describe HP in 2003 — the campus, the culture, the sense of what the company believed it was. How did that compare to what you had seen at Agilent?
Carly Fiorina was CEO until 2005. What was the unofficial view of her strategy among the people you worked with — what did people say in the hallway that they would not say in the meeting?
Who was the most effective operational leader you worked under at HP between 2003 and 2008, and what made them effective at that scale?
HP had a culture of engineering excellence that predated most of its management structure. By 2005, what was the relationship between that engineering culture and the people running the company?
When did you first sense that HP was going to miss the shift from hardware to services as the primary revenue engine?
Describe a BPO customer engagement from this period. Who was the customer, what were they trying to do, and what was HP actually delivering versus what had been sold?
HP's BPO business was selling IT-as-a-service before that had a name. What was the internal conversation about whether HP was a services company or a hardware company pretending to be one?
What was the gap between what HP sold to BPO customers and what it could actually deliver, and how was that gap managed day to day?
HP acquired EDS for $14 billion in 2008 and added 140,000 employees overnight. What did that feel like from inside the company? Describe the week the news broke.
After EDS, HP was suddenly one of the largest IT employers on earth. Describe what a routine meeting felt like differently — the overhead, the layers, the velocity.
Who inside HP best understood what EDS actually was — its culture, its liabilities, its people — and was that person listened to?
EDS had a culture built around government contracts and cost-plus billing. HP had a culture built around product margins. What happened when those two cultures met at the working level?
The BPO division you managed was directly affected by the EDS integration. What decision did you have to make that year that you could not have anticipated before the acquisition?
When did you know the EDS acquisition was not going to deliver what had been promised? Was it a number, a meeting, a project that went sideways?
What was the internal narrative HP told itself about why EDS was working, and what was the version of events the people on the ground knew was true?
Describe the India operations during the EDS integration period. How did the offshore center absorb the bureaucracy that came with 140,000 new colleagues?
Mark Hurd was CEO during the EDS integration and was forced out in 2010. What was the ground-level read on his leadership style and what changed when he left?
HP acquired Autonomy for $11 billion in 2011 and wrote off $8.8 billion a year later. Describe the internal reaction when the acquisition was announced. Who was skeptical and who was not?
What did people at HP who had done software acquisition due diligence before believe about the Autonomy deal that the decision-makers apparently did not know?
Describe the atmosphere at HP in the months after the Autonomy write-down was announced. What happened to meetings? What changed in the building?
Meg Whitman took over as CEO in 2011 and inherited the Autonomy disaster. What was the operational reality she walked into and what did the people around her understand that she was still learning?
By 2012 splitting HP into two companies was being discussed internally. When did you first hear that conversation and how serious did it seem?
Autonomy was an accounting fraud. From inside HP's services division, what were the signs visible to operational people that something was structurally wrong with the acquisition thesis?
What was the specific moment you decided to retire? Was it a meeting, a number, a conversation, a realization about where the company was going?
Who was the last person at HP you truly respected and what were they still trying to build when you left?
When you left in 2013, what was the thing you knew about HP's trajectory that you could not say out loud in an exit interview?
HP split into HP Inc. and HPE in 2015. Watching from retirement, what was your reaction? Did it feel like the right diagnosis of the problem?
HPE sold its services division — including the BPO arm you ran — into the merger that became DXC Technology in 2017. What did you think when you saw that?
Looking back across forty years, what is the one structural truth about large IT organizations that every competent operational person knows and that management consistently refuses to acknowledge?
Name one person from across your entire career who understood what technology actually was — not as a product or a cost center, but as an operational doctrine.

If you could put one room from your career on the record — the room where something important actually happened, where the real decision was made or the real problem was visible — what room would it be?
Your father left a set of memoirs. What is the single biggest difference between the world he documented and the one you spent forty years inside?
What do you know now, in retirement in Austin, that you wish you had known in 1979 when you were managing IT for a polyethylene pipe manufacturer in Dallas?
When did you first feel like you understood what was actually happening inside a large organization — not the org chart version, but the real one?
